article_title: Sleep Brain Waves Predict Dementia Risk Decades Before Symptoms, Large Study Finds
summary: Article is substantively accurate with a single minor misattribution in the body: a claim about consumer wearables lacking validation is attributed to SciTechDaily but not supported by that source.

content_preview.title: Sleep Brain Waves Predict Dementia Risk Decades Before Symptoms, Large Study Finds
content_preview.summary: A machine-learning analysis of sleep EEG data from 7,105 adults found that each 10-year gap between brain age and chronological age raises dementia risk by 39%.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A large-scale study published in JAMA Network Open has found that fine-scale brain wave patterns recorded during sleep can predict a person's risk of developing dementia — and that a mismatch between a person's sleep-derived "brain age" and their actual chronological age is a meaningful early warning sign. For every 10-year gap in which the brain appeared biologically older than its owner, the risk of incident dementia rose by 39 percent, according to [Medical Xpress](https://medica...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured, accurate, and appropriately scoped News article. The article correctly represents the JAMA Network Open study's methodology (7,105 participants across 5 cohorts), the Brain Age Index (BAI), the hazard ratio of 1.39 (95% CI 1.21–1.59), and the key quotes from both senior and first authors. The analysis section is informative without being sensationalist.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 sources verified from disk snapshots. source-0.html.gz (medicalxpress, archive fallback): confirms 7,000 participants, 40–94 age range, BAI, dementia risk rise, Yue Leng and Haoqi Sun quotes — all verbatim and correctly attributed. source-1.html.gz (scitechdaily): confirms study details, BAI metric, 13 EEG features, author quotes — all correct. ISSUE: the article's claim that consumer-grade wearables 'have not yet been validated to produce the signal quality required for brain age estimation, according to [SciTechDaily]' is NOT supported by this source. SciTechDaily only states wearables could 'eventually be used outside clinical settings' — it says nothing about validation status or signal quality. source-2.html.gz (medicalxpress, archive fallback): confirms 'unique predictor' language, clinical use caveat, and the 7,105 participant count including age ≥18 (the article text says 'across five community-based cohort studies' without conflating the age-18 vs age-40 figures). source-3.html.gz (PubMed abstract): confirms hazard ratio 1.39 (95% CI 1.21–1.59; P<.001), all five cohort names (MESA, ARIC, FHS-OS, MrOS, SOF), follow-up ranges 3.5–17 years, and 1,082 participants who developed dementia. All statistics exactly match the article.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Core claims are all well-supported. Minor issue: the article attributes a specific limitation about consumer-grade wearable validation to SciTechDaily in the 'What We Don't Know' section, but that claim is not present in the SciTechDaily snapshot. The substance of the claim (consumer EEG wearables not yet validated for BAI) may be true but lacks a cited source in the article.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. The article correctly frames the findings as associative rather than causal, and the 'What We Don't Know' section provides appropriate epistemic caution.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate or overlapping coverage found in recent articles. Existing dementia-related articles cover different angles (blood test biomarkers, drug trials), not sleep EEG.
concerns: In the 'What We Don't Know' section, the article states 'Consumer-grade wearable devices that measure sleep, such as smartwatches and EEG headbands, have not yet been validated to produce the signal quality required for brain age estimation, according to [SciTechDaily].' The SciTechDaily source (source-1.html.gz) only says wearable devices 'could eventually be used outside clinical settings' — it makes no claim about validation status or signal quality. This is a subordinate body claim, not in the headline or lead, and the substance is reasonable, but the sourcing is incorrect.
recommendations: The unsourced wearable validation claim should be corrected via the corrections record. The study's own PubMed abstract supports the spirit of this claim (EEG requires polysomnography) but the specific framing about consumer devices is not in any cited source.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality submission with strong factual accuracy across its core claims. The single misattributed limitation note about consumer wearables does not affect the headline, summary, or lead — it is recoverable via a corrections record. Recommend APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS.
